slides.title: Take Control of Your Loops
slides.body: Loops repeat — but sometimes you need to **stop early**, **skip a step**, or just **hold a spot**. Python's `break`, `continue`, and `pass` give you that control.
slides.tip: These three tiny keywords unlock a whole new level of loop power.

slides.title: break — Exit Right Now
slides.body: Use `break` when you've found what you need and there's no point checking the rest. The loop **stops immediately** and Python moves on.
slides.tip: Charlie is never checked — break exits before reaching it.

slides.title: continue — Skip, Don't Stop
slides.body: `continue` skips the **rest of this one step** and jumps to the next item. The loop keeps going — you just filter out what you don't want.
slides.tip: Negative numbers are tossed aside; the loop runs on for the rest.

slides.title: pass — Placeholder, Nothing More
slides.body: `pass` does **absolutely nothing** — it's a legal no-op. Python requires something inside a block, so `pass` lets you write the structure now and fill it in later.
slides.tip: pass is not a loop keyword — it works inside if, functions, and classes too.

slides.title: Loop-else: The Secret Bonus
slides.body: A loop can have an `else` block that runs **only if `break` never fired** — perfect for "I searched everything and found nothing" patterns.
slides.output: grape was not in the list.
slides.tip: If break had fired, the else block would have been skipped entirely.

slides.title: You've Got Loop Superpowers
slides.body: `break` exits early, `continue` skips one step, `pass` holds a spot, and `else` knows if you broke out. You now control **exactly** how your loops behave.
slides.tip: Combine break and continue in the same loop to filter bad data and stop when you hit your goal.
